text: The robotic surgical system is used to perform a sigmoid resection, revealing numerous adhesions of the small bowel to the diseased area affected by diverticulitis.
The surgeon uses a robotic system to separate the small bowel from the colon, addressing unusual congenital attachments between the terminal ileum mesentery and the sigmoid colon.
The surgeon carefully manipulates a loop of ileum that is fused to the medial aspect of the proximal sigmoid, highlighting the complex and confusing anatomy encountered during the procedure.
The surgeon carefully navigates the surgical field, searching for natural tissue planes to avoid injuring the bowel or its mesentery during the dissection.

The surgeon encounters folded attachments between the sigmoid mesentery and the terminal ileum mesentery, a congenital anomaly. The surgeon carefully maneuvers to detach a loop of small bowel from the medial aspect of the sigmoid mesentery to improve visibility.

The surgeon has successfully separated the small bowel loop from the sigmoid colon, revealing the congenital attachment of the terminal ileum (TI) mesentery to the sigmoid colon. The TI mesentery is visibly attached to the sigmoid colon, requiring careful dissection to separate the two mesenteries.
did not have ureteral catheters in on this case. I do think it would have helped speed the confidence of dissection, particularly in a case like this.

The surgeon attempts to access the area behind the mesosigmoid, targeting the sacral promontory, which is considered a zone of safety. The sigmoid colon is visibly adhered to the right pelvic side wall, appearing as congenital attachments rather than those caused by diverticular disease.
The surgeon identifies the right ureter adjacent to the bowel, which is planned for resection, and carefully maneuvers to avoid injury while hugging the bowel closely.
The surgeon carefully advances into the true pelvis, proceeding slowly to navigate the anatomical structures.

The surgeon carefully separates the sigmoid colon from the right pelvic sidewall, ensuring the right ureter is visible and preserved. The ureter is seen crossing over the psoas muscle, while the surgeon maintains a safe posterior approach, avoiding the presacral plexus.

The surgeon navigates the mesorectal plane posterior to the rectal mesentery, with the right ureter visible along the medial aspect of the rectum. The dissection occurs in the retrorectal space, known as the 'holy plane,' similar to a total mesorectal excision (TME) for rectal cancer.
The surgeon has freed the sigmoid colon into the pelvis and is examining a thickened fold of mesentery near the root of the superior hemorrhoidal artery and inferior mesenteric artery (IMA).
The surgeon carefully navigates the known anatomical plane, identifying the left common iliac artery while searching for the left ureter.
The surgeon lifts the mesentery off the retroperitoneum, carefully unfolding it to minimize the risk of injury to the ureter or gonadal vessels.
The surgeon performs medial to lateral dissection, then transitions to lateral dissection, marking the anticipated proximal resection margin to maintain orientation during the procedure.
The surgeon identifies the left ureter and demonstrates the mobilization of the splenic flexure, showing the anatomical structures involved in this part of the procedure.

The surgeon demonstrates movement along the gonadal vessels, which can visually mimic the ureter. The splenic flexure is mobilized using four 8mm ports positioned from the left upper to right lower quadrant, with no changes to bed position or port targeting.

The surgeon maintains the original configuration with the patient in steep Trendelenburg position, left side up, and performs lateral to medial mobilization of the splenic flexure. The spleen is visible in the surgical field.

The surgeon identifies the pancreas and begins mobilizing the mesentery of the splenic flexure away from the pancreas. The pancreas becomes visible as the surgeon carefully lifts the mesentery, ensuring not to inadvertently move behind the pancreas.
The surgeon uses a previously created window above the superior hemorrhoidal vessels to take the mesentery, aligning it with the intended proximal resection margin.

The surgeon identifies the distal margin for the colectomy and prepares for transrectal extraction of the specimen. The superior hemorrhoidal vessels are preserved, and the mesentery is thinned using multiple bites with an energy device.
The surgeon cleans the upper rectum and checks perfusion before selecting the proximal and distal margins. Under white light, the surgeon divides the tissue proximally using short bursts of cautery.
The surgeon performs the distal division of the colon, using surgical instruments to carefully separate the tissue at the predetermined distal margin.

The surgeon mobilizes the rectum to a lower position, revealing dissected planes that facilitate transrectal extraction. This maneuver aids in achieving an attention-free anastomosis by allowing better manipulation of the rectum.
The surgeon uses a ring clamp to extract smaller pieces of the specimen from below, demonstrating the technique for transrectal extraction.
The surgeon cuts the specimen into smaller pieces using a ring clamp, performing the extraction from below without an assistant. Each piece is carefully cut to ensure smooth removal without trauma.

The surgeon performs a hand-sewn end-to-end anastomosis, avoiding the use of linear and circular staplers. The back row is completed with 2-0 PDS sutures, and the inner layer is being sewn with 3-0 V-Loc sutures.
I don't show the whole anastomosis as you've seen me do this numerous times before. It's just my preference for the creation of most colon and bowel anastomosis is to sew and not to staple.
The surgeon demonstrates the suture technique from the opposite side, looping the suture over the top to invert the common aneurysm opening.
The surgeon completes the anastomosis by bringing two sutures to meet in the middle, finalizing the two-layer closure technique.

The surgeon completes the second outer anterior row of 2-0 PDS sutures, bringing it back around to the original posterior row suture. The anastomosis is shown with two layers of absorbable sutures, ensuring a wide open lumen in the bowel.
The surgeon ties the final suture of the anastomosis and performs a leak test, confirming no tension or leakage in the bowel connection.
I hope you enjoyed.
